summary: The notebook demonstrates an offline-first, multimodal AI application for environmental conservation, specifically plant identification and health diagnosis. It leverages Google's Gemma 3n model to process plant images alongside structured prompts, generating species identification, disease detection, and multilingual conservation reports. The code implements a complete EcoVisionAI class that handles model initialization, multimodal inference, keyword-based result parsing, biodiversity metric calculation, and offline emergency guidance. Readers should understand how to integrate open-weight multimodal models into a practical, field-deployable workflow without relying on external APIs or internet connectivity.
competition_flow: Loads the Gemma 3n multimodal model via Kaggle Hub, processes plant images with structured prompts to extract species and health data, calculates biodiversity metrics, and exports offline-ready conservation reports.
data_reading: Reads a local image file path and passes it alongside a text prompt to the processor.
Uses a hardcoded Python dictionary to simulate a local species and disease database.
data_processing: Uses AutoProcessor.apply_chat_template for multimodal tokenization and chat formatting.
Applies simple keyword-based string parsing on model outputs to extract structured fields like species and health status.
insights: Open-weight multimodal models can be effectively deployed offline for field conservation tasks without relying on external APIs.
Structured prompting combined with lightweight keyword parsing provides a practical pipeline for extracting actionable botanical data.
Local biodiversity metrics like Simpson's Diversity Index can be computed directly from accumulated AI observations to monitor ecosystem health.
Offline-first architecture is essential for deploying AI tools in remote areas with limited or no internet connectivity.
